coredata.dc:title: Interference effect of nitrogen-doped CQDs on tailoring nanostructure of CoMoP for improving high-effective water splitting

coredata.dc:description: The construction of bifunctional electrocatalysts for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is significant for developing water splitting technology. The carbon-based non-noble metal catalysts could be regarded as a preferred alternative to Pt-based materials in water splitting, but very rarely Pt-like activity has been obtained by non-noble metal catalysts. Herein, NCQDs/CoMoP/NF bifunctional catalysts were designed and synthesized by the coupling relationships between N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and metal ions. The abundant functional groups on the surfaces of CQDs can effectively coordinate with metal ions with empty d orbitals to generate the stable composites. Meanwhile, the formation of metal nanoparticles can be restricted by the introduction of CQDs to form smaller crystals with stable nanostructures, thereby preventing the agglomeration and further growth of metal nanoparticles. The NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF electrode shows a low overpotential of 65 mV (10 mA cm−2) for HER and 370 mV (100 mA cm−2) for OER in a 1.0 M KOH system. Furthermore, the constructed NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF couple can reveal a small cell voltage of 1.75 V to reach the large current density of 50 mA cm−2.

head.ce:title: Interference effect of nitrogen-doped CQDs on tailoring nanostructure of CoMoP for improving high-effective water splitting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of bifunctional electrocatalysts for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is significant for developing water splitting technology. The carbon-based non-noble metal catalysts could be regarded as a preferred alternative to Pt-based materials in water splitting, but very rarely Pt-like activity has been obtained by non-noble metal catalysts. Herein, NCQDs/CoMoP/NF bifunctional catalysts were designed and synthesized by the coupling relationships between N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and metal ions. The abundant functional groups on the surfaces of CQDs can effectively coordinate with metal ions with empty d orbitals to generate the stable composites. Meanwhile, the formation of metal nanoparticles can be restricted by the introduction of CQDs to form smaller crystals with stable nanostructures, thereby preventing the agglomeration and further growth of metal nanoparticles. The NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF electrode shows a low overpotential of 65 mV (10 mA cm−2) for HER and 370 mV (100 mA cm−2) for OER in a 1.0 M KOH system. Furthermore, the constructed NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF couple can reveal a small cell voltage of 1.75 V to reach the large current density of 50 mA cm−2.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen, as a promising alternative to fossil fuels, has been regarded as a clean and renewable energy source [1–4]. Electrochemical water splitting is a promising pathway to achieve efficient hydrogen production, but water electrolysis usually requires high overpotentials for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to provide significant reaction rates [5–9]. Currently, Ir/Ru-based oxides [10] and Pt-based materials [11] are commonly used as benchmark electrocatalysts for HER and OER, respectively, but their high cost and scarcity restrict their application [12–23]. Along with the huge efforts in developing nonprecious metal catalysts, transition-metal phosphides [24–26], sulfides [27–29], selenides [30], and carbides [31,32] have been discovered and have received enormous interest in recent years. Generally, Transition metal phosphates (TMPs), such as Ni2P, CoP, FeP, and MoP nanoparticles, show great promise in electrocatalytic HER in part on the merit of their reversible dissociation of H2. However, their electrocatalytic performance is still restricted by low electronic conductivity, sluggish kinetics, and insufficient stability [33–36]. A comprehensive strategy to tailor the compositions and redesign the micro-nanostructures of TMPs has become an attractive way to further enhance their water splitting performance. However, their electrocatalytic performance is still restricted by low electronic conductivity, sluggish kinetics and insufficient stability [37–40]. A comprehensive strategy to tailor the compositions and the micro-nanostructures of TMPs has become an attractive way to further enhance their water splitting performance.

The electrocatalytic properties of TMPs can be further enhanced by introducing carbon-based support since it can prevent the aggregation of metal particles and speed up the reaction kinetics by improving the intrinsic activity and electronic structure of active sites thereon [41]. Carbon nanotube, porous carbon, graphene and carbon quantum dots have attracted increasing attention for applications in which they have been widely used in the construction of catalysts for HER. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with sizes smaller than 10 nm have attracted broad attention because of their fast electron-transfer and large electron storage ability [42–45]. In addition, CQDs can be doped with heteroatoms to form abundant surface functional groups (N, O, S, P, etc.). The electrocatalytic properties of composites can be enhanced by the heteroatom doped CQDs by boosting the electron transfer [46–48]. Besides, the functional groups thereon CQD surface are favorable for the preparation of multi-component and high-performance composite materials via anchoring sites and active sites. The N-CQDs supported flower-shaped MnO2 nanocatalysts were synthesized by Tian et al. using a microwave-assisted method [49]. The conductivity and the specific surface area of the catalyst was raised by splitting a large MnO2 nanoflower into several small nanoflowers with rough surface. The strongly coupled CQDs/MoP nanohybrids was developed by Zhang et al. through a charge-directed self-assembly strategy. The aggregation and the surface oxidation of MoP nanoparticles were effectively alleviated by the introduction of CQDs [50]. Although previously reported studies have demonstrated the excellent electrocatalytic properties of CQDs and metal composite matrix materials, the interaction mechanism of CQDs with metals is rarely studied. More importantly, the interference mechanism of CQDs on metal crystal growth has not yet been investigated.

In the present work, an innovative method was employed for preparing NCQDs/CoMoP/NF catalysts using N-doped CQDs from coal tar pitch. The evolution mechanism of coal tar pitch to CQDs and the effect of N atom doped on CQDs were deeply studied. The interaction effects of CQDs on metal crystal growth were also investigated. The NCQDs/CoMoP/NF catalyst exhibits excellent catalytic performance in HER, OER, and overall water splitting, and has great potential in the future.

Ni foam with a porosity of 98%, a areal density of 350 g m−2 and a pore diameter of 0.2 mm was purchased from Shenzhen Green and Creative Environmental Science and Technology Co. Ltd . All of the chemicals were used as received without any further purification. CTP was obtained from Taiyuan Coal Coking Co, Ltd. Cobalt chloride hexahydrate (Co((NO3)2•6H2O) (AR), melamine C3H6N6 (AR), ammonium molybdate ((NH4)2MoO4) (AR), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (AR), formic acid (HCOOH) (AR), ammonia (NH3•H2O) (AR) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) (AR) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co, Ltd. Sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2) (AR) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co, Ltd. The 20 wt % Pt/C catalyst (Hispec 3000) and Nafion D-520 dispersion (5.0 wt %) were obtained from Johnson Matthey and Alfa Aesar, respectively.

To obtain the N-doped pitch, 5 g of coal tar pitch and 10 g of melamine were put into a tube furnace, which was heated to 450°C at 3°C min−1 and kept at 450°C for 90 min (The preparation of CQDs does not require this process). 1 g of N-doped pitch was ground for 20 min in a mortar, which was oxidized in 150 ml of 88% formic acid (HCOOH) and 15 ml of 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The samples were filtered with 100 nm filter paper. The filtrate was dialyzed in a 1000 Dalton dialysis bag for 72 h to obtain a solution of CQDs. Finally, the CQD solution was dried into a tan powder by a freeze-drying apparatus. CQDs are obtained by direct oxidation of coal tar pitch. In contrast to the preparation of N-doped CQDs, the preparation of CQDs does not require the addition of melamine during calcination.

A piece of nickel foam (1 cm × 2 cm) was washed separately with concentrated hydrochloric acid, anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for 20 minutes to ensure that its surface was well cleaned before use. Concentrated hydrochloric acid was used to remove the oxide layer on nickel foam. Anhydrous ethanol and deionized water were used to remove concentrated hydrochloric acid on nickel foam. The NCQDs/CoMoP/NF was prepared as follows: First, 2 mmol Co(NO3)2•6H2O and 2 mmol (NH4)2MoO4 were completely dissolved in 40 mL prepared carbon dots solution (Concentrations of 50, 100, and 200 mg/L) under magnetic stirring. Second, the solution was shifted to a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a piece of Ni foam. Third, the autoclave was sealed and held at 160°C for 6 h. After the autoclave was cooled at room temperature, NCQDs/CoMoO4/NF was collected and washed with water several times and then dried at 60°C for several hours. Finally, NCQDs/CoMoP/NF was placed in the hot center of the tube furnace and an alumina boat containing about 2 g of sodium hypophosphite powder was placed at the farthest upstream position in the tube furnace, which was then heated from room temperature to 450°C at 3°C min−1 under N2 and held at 450°C for 120 min, followed by cooling to room temperature. Using deionized water instead of the CQDs dot solution, CoMoP/NF was prepared under the same conditions. 10 mg commercial Pt/C or RuO2 and 5 μL 5 wt% Nafion solution were dispersed in 2 mL 1:1 v water/ethanol solvent by 30 min sonication to form an ink. Then the catalyst ink was coated on Ni foam with the same loading with NCQDs/CoMoP/NF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: During the calcination process, the coal tar pitch is dehydrated and condensed at high temperatures to form coke with a certain orientation through π-π stacking, and N atoms are doped into the coke [51]. During the oxidation process, the macromolecules of the coke are tailored into small molecules to become NCQDs (Fig. 1 a) [52]. The changes in chemical structure among coal tar pitch, N-doped coke, and NCQDs are shown by infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 1b). The absorption band at 3100-2800 cm−1 assigned to the C-H stretching vibration of aromatic and aliphatic hydrogen, gradually decreases from the original coal tar pitch to N-doped coke, indicating the loss of hydrogen atoms during the process and the increase in the degree of aromaticity. The hydrogens on an aromatic ring are divided into isolated hydrogens (in the range of 890-870 cm−1), two adjacent hydrogens (850 and 810 cm−1) and more than three adjacent hydrogens (790-720 cm−1), in order to suggest the degree of side-chain substitution [53]. It is obvious that the absorption band of N-doped coke in the range of 950-800 cm−1 is weaker than the original coal tar pitch after high temperature treatment. These results indicate that N-doped coke has a larger aromatic core and fewer aliphatic side chains compared to coal tar pitch. Comparing with N-doped coke, NCQDs have more abundant oxygen/nitrogen containing functional groups (O-H (3421 cm−1), C-O/C-C (1603 cm−1) and C-N (1356 cm−1)). Actually, the macromolecules in N-doped coke are broken down into small molecules in NCQDs could result in the breaking of the polyaromatic structure to form functional groups of NCQDs.

The main process of this reaction is the exfoliation of N-doped coke small molecules to generate NCQDs of uniform size. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Figs. 1c, d, and S1) show the uniform distribution of the NCQDs with an average diameter of 5.42 ± 0.15 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the inset of Fig. 1c indicates the presence of minority crystal lattices (d = 0.216 nm) of the as-prepared CQDs, corresponding to the (100) planar spacing of graphene carbon [54]. A broad peak centered at 22.1° is presented by the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of the NCQDs (Fig. 1e), which corresponds to to highly crystalline carbon [55].

The surface chemical states of the NCQDs and CQDs are examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy XPS (Figs. 1f, S2, and S3), and identified three typical peaks of C 1s (286 eV), O 1s (399.8 eV), and N 1s (532.5 eV) are identified. Interestingly, the NCQDs have 20.21% nitrogen content (Tables S1 and S2), obviously outperforming most nitrogen N-doped CQDs reported in the literature (Table S3). UV-Vis (Fig. 1g) spectrum shows that NCQDs have an absorption peak at 260 nm, which was attributable to typical π-π* transitions for aromatic C=C bonds and n-π* transitions for conjugated C=O and C=N bonds, respectively [56]. In the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 1g, the optimal excitation wavelength and optimal emission wavelength of NCQDs are 360 nm and 470 nm, respectively, and the Stokes shift can be calculated to be 110 nm. In the inset of Fig. 1g, blue fluorescence appears at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, indicating that the NCQDs have photoluminescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the NCQDs/CoMoP/NF
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of NCQDs/CoMoP/NF is illustrated in (Fig. 2 a). NCQDs act as electron-donor ligands that form complexes with metal ions, which hindered the agglomeration of metal nanoparticles and made metal nanoparticles uniformly distributed on the nickel foam during the low-temperature hydrothermal process. Then P was added through a phosphorylation process. The SEM images (Fig. 2b) of the NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF demonstrate that the entire surface of the Ni foam is evenly covered by the structure of the CoMoP nanoparticles and NCQDs nanosheets. Large-sized urchin-like nanotopography, with an axial length of about 3 μm and a radial diameter of about 20 μm, is presented on the surface of nickel foam in the absence of NCQDs (Fig. S4 a, b, c). The urchin-like crystal structure developed into octahedral spinel nanoparticles when the concentration of NCQDs was 50 mg/L (Fig. S4 d, e, f). Interestingly, the surface of NCQDs-50/CoMoP/NF has both rods and octahedral structure. A structure in which metal nanoparticles were encapsulated by NCQDs nanosheets was formed at a concentration of NCQDs of 100 mg/L (Fig. 2b, c, d, e). It is worth noting that the appearance of the NCQDs nanosheets makes the sea urchin-like crystal structure disappear, which indicates that the large metal particles are no longer present. The metal particle size was further reduced with the addition of excess NCQDs (Fig. S4 g, h, i). Excessive encapsulation of metal nanoparticles by NCQD nanosheets hinders the exposure of catalyst active sites. Further, the high-magnification TEM image (Fig. 2f) of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF displays CoMoP NPs confined tightly in carbon supports and shows clear lattice fringes of CoMoP (200) (blue arrows in Fig. 2f). Element mapping images (Fig. 2g) of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF show uniform distribution of Co, Mo, P, C and N, indicating that CoMoP nanoparticles are covered by external carbon nanosheets. This novel conformation is mainly due to the intense coordination interaction between NCQDs and metal cations. NCQDs contain a large number of electron donor groups(-OH, -COONH4, -NH-, etc.), which may undergo strong ion-exchange with cations of metal salt and interfere with the ion supply for crystal growth (Fig. S5), thereby hindering the formation of larger crystals. The presence of NCQDs may also provide more nucleation centers for crystal formation. Furthermore, the formation of unique core-shell structures is further induced by the spatial refinement effect of surface NCQDs during calcination [57].

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns are used to study the structural and crystalline information of the as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the diffraction peaks located at 27.4°, 39.6°, 42.9°, and 49.3° are assigned to the (011), (112), (211) and (020) planes of CoMoP (JCPDS No. 32-0300), respectively. Compared with CoMoP/NF, the CoMoP diffraction peaks of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF are significantly broadened, indicating that NCQDs can change the crystal size of CoMoP, which is consistent with the SEM analysis. Compared with the XRD standard card of CoMoP (JCPDS No. 32-0300) and CQDs (JPCDS No. 50-1364), CoMoP and NCQDs on NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF were successfully cross-linked. Fig. 3b displays the Raman spectra of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF. All samples exhibit two prominent peaks at 1370 cm−1 (D band) and 1598 cm−1 (G band) which are further confirmed introduction of NCQDs. XPS pattern (Fig. 3c) are conducted the surface chemical composition and electronic states of sample. The binding energies (BEs) of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF at 781.6, 784.2, 797.3, and 801.8 eV are correspond to Co2+ 2p3/2, Co3+ 2p3/2, Co2+ 2p1/2, and Co3+ 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 3d) [58]. The BEs of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF around 230.8, 232.1, 235.5, and 336.5 eV are attributed to Mo4+ 2d3/2,Mo4+ 2d5/2, Mo6+ 2d3/2 and Mo6+ 2d5/2, respectively (Fig. 3e). The BEs of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF at 129.5, 130.5, and 134.1 eV are attributed to P 2p3/2, P 2p1/2 and P 2p3/2, respectively (Fig. 3f).Compared with that of the CoMoP/NF, the Co 2p and Mo 3d BE of NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF shift to a higher binding energy, while P 2p BE shifts to a lower binding energy. These shifts show that a strong electronic interaction was formed between NCQDs and CoMoP in their interface [59,60]. Electrons may be transferred from Co, Mo to P, which is advantageous for producing more HER-suitable active moieties Coδ+, Moδ+ and Pδ− in the nanomaterials [61,62].

The HER performance of NCQDs-100/CoMoP (NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF) was investigated with a three-electrode system in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. Fig. 4 a shows the iR-corrected polarization curves of NCQDs-0/CoMoP, NCQDs-50/CoMoP, NCQDs-100/CoMoP, NCQDs-200/CoMoP, Pt/C and bare Ni foam. The NCQDs-100/CoMoP only needs 65 and 225 mV to drive the current density of 10 and 100 mAcm−2 in 1 M KOH (Fig. 4b), respectively, which can compete with that of commercial Pt/C (η10 = 38 mV, η100 = 190 mV). The NCQDs-100/CoMoP is much lower than NCQDs-0/CoMoP (η10 = 170 mV, η100 = 311 mV), NCQDs-50/CoMoP (η10 = 135 mV, η100 = 248 mV), NCQDs-200/CoMoP (η10 = 172 mV, η100 = 287 mV) and pure CoMoP (η10 = 183 mV, η100 = 447 mV). The significant improvement of catalytic HER activity may due to uniform-ordered carbon nanosheets formed between the CoMoP metal particles compared to the other samples. Moreover, the HER performance of NCQDs-100/CoMoP is comparable to those of the best Co- or Mo- based electrocatalysts in Table S4, e.g. FLNPC@MoPNC/MoP–C/CC (η10 = 69 mV) [63], CoP/CoMoP (η10 = 34 mV) [64], CoP-MNA (η10 = 59 mV) [65], and Co0.93Ni0.07P3/NF (η10 = 87 mV) [66], indicating the obvious advantages of carbon nanosheets wrapping CoMoP metal particles for HER. To rationally compare the catalytic performance with Pt/C, their HER catalytic activity was normalized to the current/Pt loading mass (A mg−1). The mass activities for NCQDs-100/CoMoP and Pt/C at 50 mV are 32.6 and 4.2 A mg−1, respectively, in which NCQDs-100/CoMoP shows the best mass HER activity, and 7.76 times more active than that of Pt/C [67]. The structure of carbon nanosheets wrapping CoMoP metal particles for catalyst provides multiple active sites and fast electron transfer capability.

In the Volmer step, hydrogen is adsorbed on the NCQDs-100/CoMoP (M*) surface to produce OH–and M-H*ad intermediates Eq. 1). And then, hydrogen is released from the M-H*ad intermediates active site through Heyrovsky and/or Tafel reaction (Eqs. 2-(3) [70]. The corresponding Tafel plots are shown in Fig. 4c, and the Tafel slope of 56 mV dec−1 for NCQDs-100/CoMoP is more small than that of NCQDs-0/CoMoP (132 mV dec−1), NCQDs-50/CoMoP (103 mV dec−1), NCQDs-200/CoMoP(116 mV dec−1) and pure NF (141 mV dec−1), suggesting a more efficient Volmer-Tafel mechanism obtained by using the NCQDs-100/CoMoP electrode.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements are also carried out to further understand the HER mechanism, in which the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is associated with the electrocatalytic kinetics at the interface between catalyst and electrolyte. Generally, a smaller Rct value indicates a lower charge transfer resistance and better catalytic activity. An equivalent circuit model R(CPE,R) is utilized to fit the Nyquist plots. The Rct of NCQDs-100/CoMoP is fitted to only 1.2 Ω, which is much smaller than that of NCQDs-0/CoMoP, NCQDs-50/CoMoP (2.3 Ω) and NCQDs-200/CoMoP (3.6 Ω) (Fig. 4d). Specifically, this may be attributed to the synergistic effect between MoCoP nanoparticles and NCQDs nanosheets to enhance the electrical conductivity of the composites and accelerate the electron transfer rate in the composites. Thus, the resistance of NCQDs-100/CoMoP is greatly reduced, thereby increasing the activity of hydrogen evolution. Cyclic voltammetric method is employed to evaluate the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of NCQDs-100/CoMoP, NCQDs-0/CoMoP, NCQDs-50/CoMoP and NCQDs-200/CoMoP and compared catalysts. Figs. 4e and 6 show that the Cdl values of NCQDs-100/CoMoP, NCQDs-0/CoMoP, NCQDs-50/CoMoP and NCQDs-200/CoMoP are 90.32, 358.21, 62.54 and 64.33 mF cm−2, respectively. This can be attributed to the abundant NCQDs nanosheets on MoCoP nanoparticles, which can greatly expand the surface area of the material, thereby increasing the contact area between the electrocatalyst and electrolyte. For practical application, sound stability is another critical significance of HER catalysts. Fig. 4f presents the polarisation curves of NCQDs-100/CoMoP before and after 1000 CV cycles. The catalytic performance of NCQDs-100/CoMoP after 1000 cycles merely shows a slight change compared with the initial activity, indicating its superior durability in 1 M KOH. Moreover, the current density around 10 mA cm−2 in chronoamperometry measurement (the inset of Fig. 4f) is stable with no significant change. The reason for the good stability of the sample may be that the nanosheets provide a "buffer" device for MoCoP nanoparticles, which stabilizes the connection between the metal particles and "buffers" the "deformation" of the reaction (the dissolution and aggregation of metal nanoparticles in the reaction process), thereby increasing the stability [71–73].

CQDs not doped by N were additionally synthesized (see Experimental Section). CQDs-100/CoMoP was synthesized by a process of low temperature hydrothermal and low temperature phosphating. Fig. S7 shows that the activity reduction of CQDs-100/CoMoP (η10 = 91 mV) was reduced by 26 mV compared with NCQDs-100/CoMoP (η10= 65 mV), indicating that the introduction of N atoms enhanced the activity of NCQDs-100/CoMoP against HER. The original charge distribution on the surface of the carbon layers are disorganized by defects caused by N atom. Generally, it is supposed that N-doped could markedly raise the conductivity of NCQDs and facilitate strong complexation between NCQDs and the metal cations [74,75].

The OER activity of different catalysts was evaluated in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte using a standard three electrode system. For controllable experiments, similar measurements for NCQDs-0/CoMoP, NCQDs-50/CoMoP, NCQDs-100/CoMoP, NCQDs-200/CoMoP, RuO2 and bare Ni foam were also implemented. As shown in iR-corrected polarization curves of samples (Fig. 5 a), the NCQDs-100/CoMoP electrode exhibits higher catalytic activity for OER than others. NCQDs-100/CoMoP requires an overpotential of only 370 mV to reach 100 mA cm−2, which is only 20 mV higher than that of RuO2. Moreover, the OER performance of NCQDs-100/CoMoP is comparable to those of the best Co- or Mo- based electrocatalysts in Table S5, indicating the obvious advantages of carbon nanosheets wrapping CoMoP metal particles for OER. The OER kinetics was also estimated by corresponding Tafel plots of these electrodes (Fig. 5b). The NCQDs-100/CoMoP delivers a Tafel slope of 67 mV dec−1, which is smaller than that of NCQDs-0/CoMoP (89 mV dec−1), NCQDs-50/CoMoP (78 mV dec−1) NCQDs-200/CoMoP (91 mV dec-1) and bare Ni foam (133 mV dec−1), indicating a more rapid OER rate for NCQDs-100/CoMoP. The durability of NCQDs-100/CoMoP was characterized by measuring polarization for 1000 cycles (Fig. 5c), the material gives almost the same polarization curve before and after scanning. Furthermore, the long-term electrochemical stability of NCQDs-100/CoMoP electrode was also probed in 1.0 M KOH (Fig. 5d). The excellent OER activity of NCQDs-100/CoMoP is accord with the above XPS result. After introducing NCQDs, more Coδ+, Moδ+ and Pδ- exposure, surface of CoMoP tends to form phosphides, and these formed species are the active sites of the OER [76]. Then, the structural stability of NCQDs-100/CoMoP after the OER test is examined by using XPS. The chemical states of elements in NCQDs-100/CoMoP after OER test is also studied. As illustrated in Fig. S8, the XPS spectrum reveals that the main components of NCQDs-100/CoMoP after the OER are intact. In addition, CQDs-100/CoMoP was synthesized by a process of low temperature hydrothermal and phosphating same as HER. Fig. S9 shows that the activity reduction of CQDs-100/CoMoP (η100 = 420 mV) was reduced by 50 mV compared with NCQDs-100/CoMoP (η10 = 370 mV), demonstrating that the introduction of N atom enhanced the activity of NCQDs-100/CoMoP for OER.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall water splitting activity and stability
ce:section.ce:para: Given that NCQDs-100/CoMoP exhibits remarkable bifunctional electrocatalytic property for OER and HER, a device for overall water splitting was assembled using NCQDs-100/CoMoP as both anode and cathode. The system affords a current density of 50 mA cm−2 at ∼1.75 V (with iR-correction) (Fig. 6 a), which is only 40 mV higher than that of Pt/C | RuO2. Finally, the NCQDs-100/CoMoP electrodes exhibit excellent stability for 24 h electrolytic reaction (Fig. 6b). Furthermore, vast generated bubbles are easily released from the electrode surface, as displayed in the optical image (Fig. 6c). The results indicate that the NCQDs-100/CoMoP is competent as a bifunctional electrocatalyst in which the high-activity comes from the synergetic effects between MoCoP nanoparticles and the NCQDs nanosheets and the smooth mass transfer benefits from the the unique structure of the three-dimensional nanosheets array.

In summary, the novel N-doped CQDs from coal tar pitch was designed and synthesized by calcination-oxidation method, which cross-linked with metal ions to form NCQDs/CoMoP/NF bifunctional catalysts. The NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF electrode shows a low overpotential of 65 mV (10 mA cm−2) for HER and 370 mV (100 mA cm−2) for OER in a 1.0 M KOH system. Furthermore, the constructed NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF couple can reveal a small cell voltage of 1.75 V to reach the large current density of 50 mA cm−2 at an alkaline electrolyte. The high performance of the NCQDs-100/CoMoP/NF bifunctional catalyst was attributed to the following aspects: (1) NCQDs can control morphology of CoMoP nanoparticles and prevent the over-aggregation of metal nanoparticles, which provides a larger electrochemically active surface area for the catalyst. (2) The abundant functional groups on the surfaces of CQDs can effectively coordinate with metal ions with empty d orbitals to generate the stable CQDs-metal ion coordination composites, resulting in improved electrochemical stability of the material. (3) N-doped of CQDs can change the surface charge distribution of the carbon layer, which significantly improves the electrical conductivity of the material. This study provides a potential approach for the synthesis of N-doped CQDs and the design of transition metal phosphate nanoparticles.
